title: Stuart Russell - AI: What if we succeed? - YouTube
text:
in December last year the now minister
of research and higher education OD
moonh together with the Minister of
Justice and public security visited San
Francisco to look into
AI a highlight of this trip was the half
day they spent at the Cav Center for
ethics science and the public at the
University of California
Berkeley there they met Professor Stuart
Russell who coach chairs this C
Center Professor Russell is one of the
world's leading researchers in Ai and I
am proud to present him as today's
keynote
speaker he's also a professor and
formerly chair of electrical engineering
and Computer Sciences at UC Berkeley and
professor of computational Precision
Health at UC San
Francisco Professor Russell we are
looking very much forward to hearing
your thoughts on the development of of
AI and the importance of researchers
engagement with the public the floor is
[Applause]
yours so thank you very much for the
introduction I am honored to be here and
I'm really looking forward to the
conversations that we're going to have
today
so let's begin at the beginning what is
artificial
intelligence uh so obviously it's about
making intelligent machines uh and
that's been a dream of mankind for at
least 2 and a half thousand
years what we have
learned uh about what that
means is this notion which was borrowed
actually from economics and philosophy
the notion of the rational
uh an entity that is intelligent to the
extent that its actions can be expected
to achieve its
objectives and this is a way in which we
measure the rationality of human beings
uh and we can also apply that concept to
machines uh so just to give you some
examples right so Alpha go which which
plays the game of Go uh has the
objective of winning the game uh and it
is trained to do that and does its best
uh when you are driving a car and you
use your navigation software uh the
objective there is to reach the
destination uh at the uh the least cost
and so the AI part of that finds the
shortest route to the destination uh
something that people are doing now uh
is actually creating fully automated
corporations uh and they simply give the
objective of generating Financial return
uh to the
shareholders so so this sounds very
reasonable right we're we're very much
in control we're specifying the
objectives uh and off we
go um but AI has a more ambitious
objective than that uh to create general
purpose systems systems that can uh
exceed human intelligence along every
relevant Dimension that whatever uh
environment you put them in whatever the
objective is they can quickly learn to
do uh a superhuman job of achieving that
objective
and here's here's a question which five
years ago nobody would have
asked but recently uh my co-author Peter
norvig uh who with whom I wrote The
Textbook uh published a paper saying
that we have
succeeded that in fact um the systems we
have now now uh like gp4 and
Gemini are real general purpose
intelligence in the same sense that the
right Brothers airplane was a real
airplane right airanes got bigger they
got more comfortable they have better
alcohol but it's the same basic idea uh
and his argument is we already have the
ideas that we need we're just going to
scale it up make it bigger and more
comfortable I disagree with that uh
vly um I actually think there are major
breakthroughs that still have to
happen I would say that uh these systems
like gp4 and Gemini the GPT like models
are a piece of this
puzzle but the problem is we don't
understand how they work we do not know
the internal principles by which they're
able to generate uh the intelligent
behavior that they
exhibit so we're in this very odd
situation where we've made a scientific
advance but we don't know what it
is uh and that makes it difficult to
build on it because we don't know what
pieces are missing uh we don't know
exactly what the capabilities of these
systems are that could be coupled with
other parts to make a a real General
purpose AI system so we're a little bit
like the situation coming back to
alcohol where you know 6 or 7,000 years
ago someone left a bowl of fruit out in
the sun uh and then came back and drank
it and got completely drunk uh and they
wow this is marvelous but we have no
idea what's going on and how it works
that's sort of the situation we're in
right
now so let's go back a little bit and
say okay how now we know what AI is how
do we make it um so an AI system very
simply is a box that takes input from
its environment and produces
Behavior and the question is what goes
in this box so that the behavior is
actually
intelligent and there have been a number
of answers during the history of AI
here's one from the
1950s that we fill the box with Fortran
programs how many people recognize
Fortran programs
a few yes the Bold ones um
so uh and and what they would do they
would fill it with Fortran programs and
then do Evolution with those Fortran
programs so they would make mutations to
the programs they would take pairs of
programs and mix them together to
produce new programs um and this worked
absolutely
terribly uh it completely failed to
produce intelligent
Behavior on the other hand um they were
using about a billion billion times less
computation than we used
today and we actually don't know what
would happen if we tried this again uh
today so for most of the history of the
field we adopted uh an approach that was
very much uh an engineering
approach we tried to understand the
principles by which uh intelligence
Behavior can be produced uh and then
turn those principles into artifacts for
reasoning for planning decision making
learning and so on and the key part of
this period of AI was the idea that that
AI systems should know things that
knowledge could be represented in
mathematical logic or in uh more General
uh probability Theory um and that
technology actually produced some really
wonderful artifacts
but around
2010 uh that all
changed and uh a technology that had
been
gestating uh since the
1950s suddenly
exploded and that technology we call
Deep learning uh and there we simply
fill the box with
circuit and um so each of these nodes is
connected to other nodes by little
connection
uh and the strengths of those
connections are adjustable and by
adjusting the strengths of those
connections we change the behavior that
comes out the end of the circuit so it's
a very very simple principle but to give
you a sense of scale if we uh took um
gp4 on the scale that I'm showing you
here on the screen which is sort of like
a chain link fence um it would be about
100 km by 100 km so it would cover you
know the whole of the southern part of
Norway um but the principle actually is
even simpler than the one we did with
the Fortran programs there we did small
random mutations and uh Crossover with
pairs of programs here we just do the
small random mutations about a
quintilian small random perturbations of
those connection strengths uh to get the
behavior to fit uh the training data
that we Supply and that could be for uh
training data with images recognizing
what's in the images it could be
recognizing the content of speech or
with the large language models it's
predicting the next word in a sequence
of
words so deep learning has
produced some amazing advances and I'll
just talk about a few of them uh
probably the most significant from the
point of view of science is uh a near
perfect solution of the protein folding
problem so the problem here is that we
we know for any protein uh the sequence
of amino acids uh that constitute that
protein uh but we don't know how to
predict the structure and until recently
the way you did that was x-ray
crystallography which was very difficult
expensive time consuming typically one
PhD per protein um and it didn't work
for a lot of proteins that can't be
crystallized or whose structure is
significantly Modified by the
crystallization process um and I'm sure
there are Chemists in the audience who
can tell me that I'm not describing this
correctly um but uh the the effort by
Deep Mind with the alpha fold sequence
of systems uh produced predictions Based
on data uh and machine learning uh that
is sufficiently accurate that for
millions of proteins we now can predict
their structure without doing those
x-ray crystallography experiments um and
biologists tell me that it's like a new
Candy Store that they get to go and play
in a related but uh quite different uh
Advance is the use of deep learning for
the simulation of physical
systems so here actually predicting how
a physical system will evolve over time
or other uh properties for example the
um stresses and strains uh in a
mechanical system here uh showing part
of uh simulation of a uh covid virus
interacting with uh aerosol water
droplets um my understanding is that
this is about a billion atom system and
uh able to simulate
this interaction uh fast enough to
iterate uh uh many times in a day
whereas uh I think with classical uh
simulation systems this would have taken
years of computation time and simulation
is an absolutely central part of many uh
uh many branches of engineering uh so
simulation of structures allows us to
evolve structures so going back to to
the same idea uh that we saw with the
Fortran programs instead of programs
here were evolving physical structures
to optimize for weight and strength and
resistance to uh to various forces and
interestingly we see that the structures
that evolve actually look more like
biological systems than like mechanical
systems I think we're also going to see
significant advances in robotic so
here's a warehouse robot that Amazon is
trying out uh they call it supporting
the human Workforce but actually if you
look at it it's doing the same job as
the person uh so Amazon has over a
million people uh doing this kind of
work in warehouses uh and the goal is
quite clear to replace them with
machines so that uh reducing cost and
increasing
capacity another kind of robot that uh
we have been waiting for for a long time
is the self-driving car uh and in San
Francisco uh it's now common tourists
used to come and ride on the cable car
in San Francisco uh now people come and
ride on the automated taxi in San
Francisco uh so it's become a relatively
normal part of life uh you just you have
an app you call the taxi it comes with
no driver delivers you to where you want
to go um but this is partly because San
Francisco has been completely mapped in
3D um and there's an enormous amount of
background computational work that is
not done by the taxi itself that enables
a taxi to drive successfully but even so
every so often something like this
happens where that here the taxi drove
into some wet cement uh and got
stuck and and this is the problem why
have been why have we been waiting so
long because to drive
successfully you need about eight nines
of
reliability and getting 8 nines of
reliability means dealing with all of
these strange Corner cases that happen
in the real world uh and the AI systems
are simply not up to the job as yet um
so in controlled environments this can
work but in completely unstructured
environments uh and certainly uh in
cities that are less well mapped and
less well
signposted uh than San Francisco I think
we are still years away from
that so among some of my colleagues
there's the view that deep learning as I
said you know is already the answer to
all the questions but in
fact uh there are reasons to think that
that deep learning as it's practiced
actually doesn't solve uh the problems
of AI and here I'm going to get a little
bit
nerdy um
and say look if you have a circuit like
the one I showed you in the box right
it's what we call a linear time
computation system so the amount of
computation that gets done is exactly
proportional to the size of the circuit
because the signal comes in at one end
passes through the layers of the circuit
comes out the other
end so what does that mean if you're
trying to solve a computational task
that cannot be solved in linear time uh
and there's a whole category of problems
we call the NP hard problems where we
know at least we're very very confident
that they require exponential time to
solve so what that means is the only way
that deep learning can solve those
problems is for the circuit itself to be
exponentially
large and that means that it's going to
require an exponential amount of data to
train that circuit
and you now have an exponentially doed
circuit which requires a huge amount of
uh energy as the Prime Minister
mentioned uh to do those
computations uh and so in practice
rather than build exponentially large
circuits which would be vaster than the
universe uh we end up approximating
these functions the systems are not
actually able to learn these functions
correctly even though they're very
simple to
describe they systems are not able to
learn them and I'll give you an example
so in
2017 alphago defeated the human world
champion uh his name is k from China and
this was regarded as China's Sputnik
moment this was the moment where China
decided that it needed to lead the world
in AI otherwise it would be left
behind and
since then go programs have gone far
beyond the human
level so uh right now the best program
uh is called catago this is a particular
version called jbx
c005 its rating is 5,200 the human world
champion rating is about
3,800 so it's massively superhuman it
would expect to win 99 or 100 times out
of a 100 against the human world
champion but I'm going to show you a
game between uh a graduate student from
Montreal who's a member of our extended
research group Kellen Pelin he's an
amateur go player his rating is about
2300 and he's going to play against
katago and he's going to give katago a
nin Stone
handicap so if you know anything about
go a ninestone handicap is a massive
insult
it's treating kateo as if kateo were a
5-year-old just learning to play the
game so in go uh you take turns putting
down your Stones the idea is to surround
territory uh and to surround your
opponent's pieces if you completely
surround your opponent's pieces they are
captured and removed from the board now
you know how to play go so let's look at
the
game so remember that the computer's
playing Black starts with this Mass
advantage and uh Kellen is playing white
and keep an eye on what happens in the
bottom right quadrant of the board so
white is beginning to make a little tiny
group of stones and then black very
quickly surrounds that group to stop it
from
growing and now white starts to surround
the black
group and interestingly black does not
noticed this black has many
opportunities to rescue those stones and
connect them up to the rest of the board
but simply fails to observe that it's
going to lose those stones unless it
does something and then it loses those
stones uh and loses the
game so this is an elementary failure by
A system that is supposedly superhuman
and the reason seems to be that it has
failed to learn the basic concept of go
which is a group of stones it doesn't
seem to understand that that group of
stones is a group maybe because of a
circle uh we actually trained a version
of Catos specifically to recognize this
problem uh so we gave it lots and lots
of examples of circular groups where uh
we were about to capture them and then
it learned finally uh to defend those
circular groups but then lo and behold
when you make a group in the shape of a
figure of eight again it doesn't
recognize that that's a group of stones
and allows us to capture it
and so uh there's a fundamental failure
here of the deep Learning Systems to
learn relatively simple Concepts
correctly and we see this with the large
language models which despite millions
of examples have not learned to do
arithmetic so they cannot add and
multiply numbers of arbitrary size and
in fact what it looks like is that each
time we make the network 10 times bigger
they get one digit better at doing
arithmetic
and so that's the characteristic one
would expect from a lookup table rather
than from a system that's actually
learned the concept
correctly so at the moment um and here's
a diagram taken from uh a pretty
important document called situational
awareness by Leopold Ashen brener who
was at open AI um the leading companies
and the experts within them are
predicting super human AI before the end
of this
decade uh and they based this on what
they call scaling laws which refer to
the Improvement in performance as a
function of the amount of data and the
number of parameters and the amount of
computation that's
done and here are some reasons I think
which would suggest that this will
happen one is that the R&D budget is
about 10 times the size of the Manhattan
Project
uh if you're a
physicist uh working on uh Nuclear
Physics these days it's 100 times the
annual budget of the Large Hadron
kollider
project so that gives you a sense of
scale uh and that that money causes very
many smart people to be working in this
area um and I think many of them realize
the uh the weaknesses of Simply SC
scaling up these giant circuits uh and
they are trying lots of other ideas so I
would not uh want to rule out the
possibility that further breakthroughs
will
occur on the other hand uh we are
running out of real
data so we probably maybe get one more
order of magnitude of scaling of
language models but beyond that we have
used up all the text in the
universe a second reason why it might
not happen is that the investors run out
of patience that's already happened with
many of the self-driving car companies
where the progress did not justify
further
investment uh according to Goldman Sachs
there's about $500 billion that has been
plowed into creating AGI so far and at
some point people will
say Where's the Beef right and uh and if
they lose patience then we could see
another AI winter uh much bigger than
the one we experienced in the in the
late
1980s so I'm not going to give you a
prediction about which of these
eventualities I think will come to pass
um but I think we have to uh acknowledge
that whether it happens in this decade
or in some future decade we are going to
succeed in creating superhuman general
purpose
Ai and it's worth remembering why we
were doing this in the first place
besides the fact that it's really
cool um if we had general purpose AI
then by
definition we could do what human beings
have already managed to
do uh and as I mentioned last night uh
our Ingenuity as humans has created a
civilization where hundreds of millions
of people get to enjoy uh an objectively
Splendid style of life as I put it
but we could use AI to do that for
everybody because we can do it at much
greater scale and at much lower
cost and that would be roughly a 10-fold
increase in the GDP of the world and the
net present value of that so the cash
equivalent therefore the a lower bound
on the technology value of AGI would be
15 quadrillion
dollars so that gives you a sense of
what is pulling us forward a giant
magnet in the future and the closer we
get the stronger the force from that
magnet and of course we could do more
things we might have much better health
much better education much better
science almost any other thing you can
think of so potentially a golden age for
Humanity um an obvious issue is what are
human beings going to do when AI systems
are doing everything for them this is
from Wally uh and and pointing out one
obvious
problem um this is Alan Turing he
pointed out another obvious problem back
in
1951 he said it seems probable that once
the machine thinking method had started
it would not take long to outstrip our
feeble Powers at some stage therefore we
should have to expect the machines to
take
control
okay and I think uh as uh as the Prime
Minister mentioned this is the question
that he had in his
mind how do we retain power over
entities more powerful than us
forever and when you ask a question that
way it actually seems a little difficult
right we don't have a lot of good
examples of this happening in the
past but I think it's better to ask it a
slightly different way because we get to
choose how we design the AI
systems so what
is a mathematically defined problem how
do we set up the AI problem such that if
the machine solves it no matter how well
it solves it we are guaranteed to be
happy with the
result so this is what I've been working
on now for about a
decade and um it's not the things we've
been
doing the standard model that I
mentioned at the the beginning where you
provide a fixed objective to the system
uh and it optimizes that objective uh
that does not work when you go into the
real
world it's impossible to specify the
objective
correctly it's the king what we call the
King Midas problem King Midas wanted
everything he touched to turn to Gold
that was the objective that he stated
the gods Grant his objective here
they're playing the role of the AI
system and of course his food and his
drink and his family all turn to gold
and and then he dies in misery and
starvation and there are many many other
Legends like this in many many cultures
around the world that uh be careful what
you wish
for what we're doing with large language
models is actually even worse we're not
specifying the objective we're simply
training them to copy the behavior of
human beings and I'll show you later why
that's a really bad idea
so
instead The Proposal is
that machines have to act in the best
interest of
humans and that itself is a complicated
question so I put two asterisks there
meaning this is a complicated
question um but the important thing is
those machines are explicitly uncertain
about what those human interests are and
this is a signic ific departure from the
way that we have designed AI systems in
the past where we always specify the
objectives so every chapter of my
textbook talks about how the objectives
are specified and then how you design
algorithms to achieve that type
objective of objective under those kinds
of conditions but here we're beginning
from a different premise where the
objective itself is unknown and
therefore it's the job of the system but
both to find out more about what it is
the humans want but also to behave then
it's going to behave
cautiously because it knows that there
are things it doesn't know about what
humans want and therefore it should not
mess with the universe uh unless it's
pretty sure that that's how we want the
universe messed
with and we can turn this into a
mathematical framework that we call an
assistance game so it falls within the
economic framework of Game Theory which
is decision problems involving two or
more entities so here there's at least
one human and at least one machine the
human presumably has some preferences
about the future they may not be
complete and the human may not even know
their own preferences um and then
there's a machine whose job it is to
help satisfy the human preferences even
though it starts out not knowing what
those preferences are and I think I hope
that from this explanation you can see
that the better the AI systems solves
this problem the happier we are going to
be because that uh that basic issue of
misalignment does not
arise and we can see by looking at by
solving assistance games and looking at
the solutions and analyzing the
mathematics of this
framework that without having to be told
these AI systems will defer to humans
they will act cautiously so this idea of
minimally invasive Behavior they will
change as little as possible about the
world uh and they will always be willing
to be switched off this is a
mathematical theorem they have a
positive incentive to allow humans to
switch them off uh and that's I think
crucial to retaining control forever and
we can show that in fact in our best
interests to build assistance game
solvers even if we don't know what our
own interests are we should still build
these things but they have to be bu very
carefully uh and there's a lot more
theory that I haven't explained
here so over the past uh couple of years
here are some of the specific questions
that we worked on uh the first one is
what happens when uh either the human or
the machine or both does not have full
access to the state of the world uh and
if you were ever a child I assume most
of you were children at some point and
if your parents ever told you to tidy up
your bedroom which I assume they did uh
you probably figured that you could just
shove everything under the bed and your
parents wouldn't know right we don't
want the AI systems to shove everything
on the bed uh and try to fool the humans
so we have to make sure that that's not
the solution to the assistance game uh
when there is partial observability and
indeed it turns out that the AI systems
will not shove everything under the bed
uh because then there's no guarantee
that the humans believe that the AI
system has actually solved the
problem uh we want to scale these things
up so they can work on bigger and bigger
problems scale up to the real world uh
so we've got as far as Minecraft so now
uh you can have a little helper that is
watching you while you're doing
Minecraft and actually maybe even
signaling uh with you to try to figure
out more about what you want uh and
we'll quickly figure out what you're
trying to do uh and help you do it
um one of the most fundamental questions
is this issue of plasticity of human
preferences so the fact that uh human
preferences are not
fixed and we don't actually sort of
autonomously choose our
preferences right in fact the
preferences we have about the future are
not the ones we were born with they are
the result of our upbringing peer
pressure our culture everything else as
well as our
biology
um and so that means that AI systems
themselves can manipulate our
preferences and I believe this is one of
the things that's happening with social
media for example so how do we make sure
that the solution to the assistance game
does not uh involve AI systems that
manipulate human preferences they can't
avoid affecting our preferences
because any form of interaction with
humans is going to affect our
preferences particularly one uh that
involves a super helpful household
Butler that does everything for you uh
and helps you run your life of course
that's going to change your preferences
um but how do we make sure that it
doesn't do it in a a manipulative
way uh and then the last thing actually
Hawks back to uh what I was mentioning
about the approach to AI based on
knowledge was prevalent from let's say
1965 uh until
2010 um
so during that period this was simply an
article of faith that intelligent
systems should know things that they
should have internal knowledge that is a
representation of the external world
that's what science does in fact is
create those internal representations
for the human race of the external world
so so that we can then uh reason and
plan using that
knowledge but why is that a good
idea why don't we simply learn an input
output
mapping now we know that for example for
NS and Crosses or
tic-tac-toe it's a perfectly sensible
solution just to build a lookup table
saying if this is the position I'm in
then this is the right
move and that lookup table doesn't know
the rules of the game it doesn't know
what the objective is uh but it still
plays
perfectly so the question is why do we
think that knowing things about the
world is a good
idea and so the fundamental theorem of
AI would be an answer to that
question and we're making some progress
towards that and actually showing that
for a very large class of mathematically
defined uh
environments the sample complexity
meaning the rate of
learning for uh knowledge-based systems
is exponentially better than it is for
simple input output
systems um and so if we can take this uh
F this work further and generalize those
results that would actually be the basis
of a beginning of a real scientific
theory of intelligence in my
view there's a lot more to do we have to
understand much more about real humans
if AI systems are really going to learn
about human
preferences and uh there still remain
questions uh in Social Choice Theory how
you make decisions on be on behalf of
many human
beings and these problems in Social
Choice Theory go back thousands of
years they involve uh the theory of
utilitarianism which basically says
everyone's preferences count equally so
you add them up
um but then there are issues about
egalitarianism there are issues to do
with decisions that affect how many
people
exist uh and many other open

of the dangers of achieving AGI and a

Solutions
the difficulty we face is that it's hard
to Define safe and beneficial for

hit the ground until it's supposed
to right so it's a fairly simple
definition of safety for a nuclear power
station uh we don't want it to explode
and we don't want it to release
radiation but for AI systems because
they're so
General uh the definition of safety is
quite amorphous and
and culturally varied geographically
varied uh and very difficult to
Define so instead we can actually
Define obviously unsafe behaviors so
they're way at the unsafe end they're
precisely defined we call these red
lines and the onus should be on the

medicines and airplanes and buildings
and uh nuclear power stations the owners
of proof is on the developer to show
that their system is not going to cross

they said to be well defined ideally
they should be automatically detectable
we also want them to be politically
feasible so that the Prime Minister can

difficult for for the companies to say
to get up in front of Congress or

guarantee that our systems are not going
to advise terrorists on how to build

indefensible
position and so uh I believe we need
these red lines the effect would be that
the companies would have to do the
safety engineering that they have failed
to do here's the situation that the
companies are
in right imagine if the airplane
manufacturers had decided instead of
doing all the engineering and the

breed bigger and bigger
birds and then they come to the uh
Federal Aviation Administration and say
well we'd like you to certify this

produced by doly this picture um and
that's why the bird has three
legs and I think the FAA would say look
these birds eat the passengers they drop
them in the ocean we are not certifying
your technology come back when you have
